       AVG(g.exam_score) AS avg_exam_score,
       COUNT(g.id) AS grade_count
FROM public.grades g
JOIN public.enrollments e ON g.enrollment_id = e.id
JOIN public.course_sections cs ON e.course_section_id = cs.id
JOIN public.professors p ON cs.professor_id = p.id
WHERE p.academic_title IS NOT NULL
  AND g.exam_score IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY p.academic_title
ORDER BY avg_exam_score DESC, p.academic_title ASC;","validationIssues":["All encoded fields (academic_title, avg_exam_score, grade_count) exist in metadata.columns and are returned by the SQL query.","The y axis scale domain is fixed to [0,100]; confirm exam_score is bounded to that range or remove/adjust the domain to avoid clipping or misleading scale.","Color encodes avg_exam_score but no legend is shown and it is redundant with bar height; consider removing color or adding a legend if you intend color as a separate channel.","The SQL orders by avg_exam_score DESC then academic_title ASC; the Vega-Lite sort uses avg_exam_score DESC but does not implement the secondary alphabetical tie-breaker, so tie ordering may differ.","Academic titles are treated as raw categories. visualization.title: Average exam score by professor academic title

resultRaw.sql:
SELECT p.academic_title AS academic_title,
       AVG(g.exam_score) AS avg_exam_score,
       COUNT(g.id) AS grade_count
FROM public.grades g
JOIN public.enrollments e ON g.enrollment_id = e.id
JOIN public.course_sections cs ON e.course_section_id = cs.id
JOIN public.professors p ON cs.professor_id = p.id
WHERE p.academic_title IS NOT NULL
  AND g.exam_score IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY p.academic_title
ORDER BY avg_exam_score DESC, p.academic_title ASC;

issues: All encoded fields (academic_title, avg_exam_score, grade_count) exist in metadata.columns and are returned by the SQL query.
The y axis scale domain is fixed to [0,100]; confirm exam_score is bounded to that range or remove/adjust the domain to avoid clipping or misleading scale.
Color encodes avg_exam_score but no legend is shown and it is redundant with bar height; consider removing color or adding a legend if you intend color as a separate channel.
The SQL orders by avg_exam_score DESC then academic_title ASC; the Vega-Lite sort uses avg_exam_score DESC but does not implement the secondary alphabetical tie-breaker, so tie ordering may differ.

Academic titles are treated as raw categories. If the question intends a hierarchical comparison of "higher" titles, consider normalizing titles to a rank/order to reflect seniority rather than relying on string ordering.
Some title categories have small grade_count (sample shows counts as low as 2), so means may be unreliable — consider showing sample sizes prominently or confidence intervals.

visualization.title: Average exam score by professor academic title

result.sql:
SELECT p.academic_title AS academic_title,
       AVG(g.exam_score) AS avg_exam_score,
       COUNT(g.id) AS grade_count
FROM public.grades g
JOIN public.enrollments e ON g.enrollment_id = e.id
JOIN public.course_sections cs ON e.course_section_id = cs.id
JOIN public.professors p ON cs.professor_id = p.id
WHERE p.academic_title IS NOT NULL
  AND g.exam_score IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY p.academic_title
ORDER BY avg_exam_score DESC, p.academic_title ASC;

issues: All encoded fields (academic_title, avg_exam_score, grade_count) exist in metadata.columns and are returned by the SQL query.
The y axis scale domain is fixed to [0,100]; confirm exam_score is bounded to that range or remove/adjust the domain to avoid clipping or misleading scale.
Color encodes avg_exam_score but no legend is shown and it is redundant with bar height; consider removing color or adding a legend if you intend color as a separate channel.
The SQL orders by avg_exam_score DESC then academic_title ASC; the Vega-Lite sort uses avg_exam_score DESC but does not implement the secondary alphabetical tie-breaker, so tie ordering may differ.

Academic titles are treated as raw categories. If the question intends a hierarchical comparison of "higher" titles, consider normalizing titles to a rank/order to reflect seniority rather than relying on string ordering.
Some title categories have small grade_count (sample shows counts as low as 2), so means may be unreliable — consider showing sample sizes prominently or confidence intervals.
